title: Convex Hull
description: Given n distinct points in the plane with positive hull area, compute the convex hull and print all points on the hull. First print k, the number of hull points, then k lines with the coordinates of those points in any order. Include all points that lie on the boundary of the hull (including points along edges).

examples.explanation: The hull corners are (2,1), (2,5), (4,4), (6,3). There are no other boundary points along edges.

mcqChain.questionText: What exactly must the output contain?

options.label: First k, the number of hull points; then k lines with the coordinates of all points on the convex hull boundary, in any order.
First k, then only the corner points of the hull in counterclockwise order.
Only the corner points of the hull; exclude points that lie on hull edges.
The indices of hull points in input order.

mcqChain.explanation: We must print k and then all points on the convex hull boundary in any order. Points on edges are included; ordering is not constrained.

wrongExplanations.1: Ordering is not required; also edge points must be included.
wrongExplanations.2: The problem asks to include all boundary points, not just corners.

mcqChain.hint: Read the output specification carefully: any order, include all boundary points.

mcqChain.questionText: Given points forming a triangle: (0,0), (2,0), (1,2). Which points are on the convex hull?

options.label: All three points: (0,0), (2,0), (1,2)
Only (0,0) and (2,0)
Only (1,2)
None; hull area is zero

mcqChain.explanation: A triangle's hull is the triangle itself; all three points are on the hull.

wrongExplanations.1: The top vertex (1,2) is also on the boundary.
wrongExplanations.3: The three points form a triangle with positive area.

mcqChain.questionText: Consider points: (0,0), (2,0), (1,1), (1,0). Is (1,0) part of the required output?

options.label: Yes, because it lies on the boundary edge from (0,0) to (2,0).
No, only extreme corners should be printed.
No, because it is collinear with (0,0) and (2,0).
Yes, but only if we print points in counterclockwise order.

mcqChain.explanation: All boundary points, including those on edges, must be printed. Ordering is irrelevant.

wrongExplanations.2: Collinear boundary points are required by the problem.
wrongExplanations.3: Order doesn't affect whether a point is on the hull.

mcqChain.hint: Boundary points include points on edges, not just corners.

mcqChain.questionText: Which statement about the order of printed hull points is correct?

options.label: They must be listed in counterclockwise order starting at the leftmost point.
They can be printed in any order.
They must be sorted by x then y.
They must be printed in the same order as input.

mcqChain.explanation: Any order is acceptable. There is no requirement on traversal or sorting.

wrongExplanations.0: This is a common choice but not required here.
wrongExplanations.2: Sorting lexicographically is not required by the output format.

mcqChain.hint: Check the output specification: any order is allowed.

mcqChain.questionText: A naive idea: for every pair of points, check if all other points lie on one side of the line segment. What is the time complexity?

options.label: O(n^3) because there are O(n^2) pairs and O(n) checks per pair.
O(n^2) because each point is checked once per pair set.
O(n log n) after sorting.
O(n) because each point is visited constantly many times.

mcqChain.explanation: There are ~n^2 pairs and verifying a pair takes up to n checks → O(n^3).

wrongExplanations.1: Each pair needs up to n checks; pairs are O(n^2).
wrongExplanations.2: Sorting alone doesn't give the hull; naive pair checking is costlier.

mcqChain.hint: Count pairs and the per-pair verification cost.

mcqChain.questionText: To systematically sweep the plane, which sorting of points is most useful?

options.label: Sort by x, breaking ties by y (lexicographic).
Sort by distance from the origin.
Sort by input order (no sorting).
Sort randomly to avoid bias.

mcqChain.explanation: Lexicographic sorting lets us sweep from left to right and build boundary chains consistently.

wrongExplanations.1: Distance from origin mixes left and right sides unpredictably.
wrongExplanations.2: Input order is arbitrary and breaks the sweep logic.

mcqChain.hint: We want to go from leftmost to rightmost in a consistent way.

mcqChain.questionText: Given three points o=(ox,oy), a=(ax,ay), b=(bx,by), which expression gives the signed area (positive/negative indicating turn direction)?

options.label: (ax-ox)*(by-oy) - (ay-oy)*(bx-ox)
(bx-ax)*(oy-ay) - (by-ay)*(ox-ax)
(ox-ax)*(oy-bx) - (oy-ay)*(ox-bx)
(ax+bx-2*ox)*(ay+by-2*oy)

mcqChain.explanation: The determinant (ax-ox, ay-oy) x (bx-ox, by-oy) gives the signed area (twice); sign indicates left/right turn.

wrongExplanations.1: This permutes terms inconsistently; sign and value are not the oriented area from o.
wrongExplanations.3: This is not an area/determinant; it mixes sums and products arbitrarily.

mcqChain.hint: Think determinant of vectors oa and ob.

mcqChain.questionText: What does the sign of the signed area area(o,a,b) indicate when scanning from o→a→b?

options.label: Positive: left turn; Negative: right turn; Zero: collinear.
Positive: right turn; Negative: left turn; Zero: collinear.
Positive: closer to origin; Negative: farther; Zero: same distance.
Sign has no geometric meaning, only magnitude matters.

mcqChain.explanation: The sign indicates orientation: left/right turn or collinearity.

wrongExplanations.1: This swaps left/right interpretation.
wrongExplanations.3: The sign is crucial to detect turn direction.

mcqChain.hint: Right-hand rule for 2D orientation.

mcqChain.questionText: When building a boundary chain in sorted order, what should we do if adding the next point makes a right turn?
mcqChain.diagram: Chain ... -> A -> B, next point C makes a right turn at (A,B,C).

options.label: Remove the last point from the chain and recheck (keep popping while it's a right turn).
Keep all points; right turns are fine as long as we sort.
Remove the first point in the chain.
Skip adding the new point and move on.

mcqChain.explanation: Right turns break convexity; we must backtrack by removing the last chain point until the turn is not right.

wrongExplanations.1: Right turns would indent the chain inward, breaking convexity.
wrongExplanations.3: Skipping may miss boundary points; we need to adjust the chain.

mcqChain.hint: Maintain convexity by keeping turns consistent.

mcqChain.questionText: We must include all points on hull edges. In the popping condition for a chain, which comparison keeps collinear points?
mcqChain.diagram: While len(chain)>=2 and area(chain[-2], chain[-1], p) ? 0: pop

options.label: Pop while area < 0 (strictly right turn only).
Pop while area <= 0 (right turn or collinear).
Pop while area > 0 (left turns).
Never pop; always append.

mcqChain.explanation: Using < 0 removes only right turns, leaving collinear points on the chain (hull edges).

wrongExplanations.1: This removes collinear points, which we must keep.
wrongExplanations.2: Removing left turns would invert the chain.

mcqChain.hint: We want to keep zero-area triples.

mcqChain.questionText: To get the other boundary chain after building one from left to right, how should we traverse the points?

options.label: Traverse the sorted points in reverse order to build the other chain.
Use the same left-to-right traversal and just invert the sign test.
Pick random unvisited points until all are used.
Traverse by increasing y only.

mcqChain.explanation: Building one chain forward and the other in reverse ensures we cover both sides consistently.

wrongExplanations.1: Simply inverting a sign with the same order does not properly construct the opposite boundary.
wrongExplanations.3: Sorting by y alone can interleave x positions incorrectly.

mcqChain.hint: Think symmetry: one chain left-to-right, the other right-to-left.

mcqChain.questionText: After building two chains, how do we combine them while avoiding duplicate endpoints?
mcqChain.diagram: lower: L0 ... Lm, upper: U0 ... Um (L0==Um, Lm==U0)

options.label: Concatenate as lower[:-1] + upper[:-1]
Concatenate as lower + upper
Concatenate as lower[1:] + upper[1:]
Concatenate unique sorted set of both chains

mcqChain.explanation: Slicing off the last point of each chain removes the shared endpoints before concatenation.

wrongExplanations.1: This duplicates the shared endpoints.
wrongExplanations.3: A set loses the chain order and may not preserve all edge points distinctly if mishandled; we will handle uniqueness separately.

mcqChain.hint: The first of one chain equals the last of the other, and vice versa.

mcqChain.questionText: Because points collinear with the leftmost/rightmost vertical edges may appear in both chains, how do we ensure we print each boundary point once?

options.label: Keep a seen set and build a unique list in the order of concatenation.
Trust slicing; duplicates cannot happen after removing endpoints.
Remove every second point to avoid duplicates.
Sort and then use binary search to avoid duplicates.

mcqChain.explanation: A seen set ensures each boundary point is added once even if it appears in both chains.

wrongExplanations.1: Collinear stacks at extremes can still duplicate across chains.
wrongExplanations.2: This arbitrarily deletes valid hull points.

mcqChain.hint: Deduplicate after concatenating with a hash set.

mcqChain.questionText: Implement the signed area helper and import. Choose the correct code snippet.
mcqChain.explanation: We need the determinant of vectors oa and ob. The other snippets change the reference point or compute a dot product.

wrongExplanations.2: That's a dot product, not oriented area.
wrongExplanations.3: This flips a and b, reversing the sign.

mcqChain.hint: Use vectors from o to a and from o to b.

mcqChain.questionText: Read all input efficiently and parse n. Pick the correct snippet.

options.label:
def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    it = iter(data)
    n = int(next(it))

def main():
    tokens = sys.stdin.read().splitlines()
    it = iter(tokens)
    n = int(next(it))

mcqChain.explanation: Read all tokens once, iterate them, and parse n first. Other options either mix methods or leave the iterator empty.

wrongExplanations.2: Splitting by lines then iterating tokens per line won't provide coordinate tokens directly.
wrongExplanations.3: Iterator is empty; cannot read points.

mcqChain.hint: We'll iterate tokens (x and y) after reading n.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    it = iter(data)
    n = int(next(it))

mcqChain.questionText: Parse n points and sort them lexicographically.

options.label:
pts = [(int(next(it)), int(next(it))) for _ in range(n)]
    pts.sort()

pts = [tuple(map(int, next(it))) for _ in range(n)]
    pts.sort(key=lambda p: p[1])

pts = [(int(next(it)), int(next(it))) for _ in range(n)]
    pts.sort(key=lambda p: (p[1], p[0]))

pts = [(int(next(it)), int(next(it))) for _ in range(n)]
    # no sort

mcqChain.explanation: We must take x then y tokens and sort by x then y (default tuple sort).

wrongExplanations.2: Sorting by y then x breaks the left-to-right sweep.
wrongExplanations.4: Without sorting, the sweep logic will fail.

mcqChain.hint: Default tuple sort is lexicographic by x then y.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
pts = [(int(next(it)), int(next(it))) for _ in range(n)]
    pts.sort()

mcqChain.questionText: Build the lower boundary chain (keep collinear points).
mcqChain.explanation: We pop only on right turns (<0) to preserve collinear boundary points. The pivot order must be (-2, -1, p).

wrongExplanations.1: Using <= removes collinear points, which we must keep.
wrongExplanations.3: Swapping the last two points changes the orientation test incorrectly.

mcqChain.hint: Preserve collinear; pop on right turns only.

mcqChain.questionText: Build the upper boundary chain by scanning in reverse (keep collinear points).
mcqChain.explanation: Reverse traversal and pop on right turns (<0) keeps collinear boundary points.

wrongExplanations.1: Not reversing builds the same chain twice.
wrongExplanations.2: Using <= removes collinear points from edges.

mcqChain.hint: Mirror the lower chain logic but traverse reversed(pts).

mcqChain.questionText: Combine the chains while dropping shared endpoints.

options.label: hull = lower[:-1] + upper[:-1]
hull = lower + upper
hull = lower[1:] + upper[1:]
hull = list(set(lower + upper))

mcqChain.explanation: Slicing off the last of each avoids duplicating the first/last point. We'll deduplicate other overlaps next.

wrongExplanations.1: This duplicates the shared endpoints.
wrongExplanations.3: A set discards order and may reorder points unpredictably.

mcqChain.hint: Both chains include the endpoints.

mcqChain.questionText: Deduplicate points that might appear in both chains (e.g., on vertical extremes), preserving a stable order.

options.label: unique = list(set(hull))

from collections import Counter
    unique = [p for p,c in Counter(hull).items()]

mcqChain.explanation: A seen set yields O(n) deduplication with stable order. set(hull) loses order; list 'in' is O(n^2) worst-case.

wrongExplanations.1: Order is lost; not ideal even if any order is allowed we want consistency.
wrongExplanations.2: Membership is O(n), leading to O(n^2) in worst-case n=2e5.

mcqChain.hint: Aim for O(n) and preserve the concatenation order.

mcqChain.questionText: Output the result and wire up main().

options.label:
out = []
    out.append(str(len(unique)))
    for x, y in unique:
        out.append(f"{x} {y}")
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

print(len(unique))
    print(unique)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

out = [len(unique)] + unique
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

print(len(unique))
    for p in unique:
        print(p)

# no main guard

mcqChain.explanation: We print k, then each point as 'x y', and ensure the program runs via the main guard.

wrongExplanations.1: Printing the list object doesn't match the required format.
wrongExplanations.3: Printing tuples '(x, y)' is not the specified 'x y' format; also missing main guard.

mcqChain.hint: Match the exact output format: one number, then k lines 'x y'.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
out = []
    out.append(str(len(unique)))
    for x, y in unique:
        out.append(f"{x} {y}")
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

mcqChain.questionText: Run the algorithm on points: (0,0), (2,0), (1,1), (1,0), (2,2), (0,2). How many hull points should be printed?
mcqChain.diagram: Square corners (0,0), (2,0), (2,2), (0,2); plus (1,1) inside; (1,0) on bottom edge.

options.label: 5 (the four corners plus (1,0))
4 (only the four corners)
6 (all six points)
5 (the four corners plus (1,1))

mcqChain.explanation: Corners plus the boundary point (1,0) are on the hull; (1,1) is strictly inside.

wrongExplanations.1: We must include points on edges.

mcqChain.hint: Include edge points, exclude interior points.

mcqChain.questionText:
For input:
8
0 0
0 1
0 2
3 0
3 1
3 2
1 1
2 1
How many unique hull points does the code print?
mcqChain.diagram: Two vertical columns x=0 and x=3 from y=0..2; interior points (1,1),(2,1).

options.label: 6 (all points on the outer rectangle sides: (0,0),(0,1),(0,2),(3,0),(3,1),(3,2))
4 (just the corners)
8 (duplicates included from both chains)

mcqChain.explanation: We keep collinear boundary points and deduplicate across chains, yielding 6 unique hull points.

wrongExplanations.2: We deduplicate with a seen set; no duplicates remain.
wrongExplanations.1: We must include edge points between corners as well.

mcqChain.hint: Vertical edges have three points each; all are on the boundary.

mcqChain.questionText: On the sample input from the statement, will the algorithm ever pop on a collinear triple?

options.label: No, because we use '< 0' and only pop on strict right turns.
Yes, because we use '<= 0' and pop collinear too.
Sometimes; depends on floating-point rounding.
It will pop on left turns instead.

mcqChain.explanation: We use integer arithmetic and '< 0' so collinear triples are kept.

wrongExplanations.1: We deliberately avoided '<=' to keep boundary points.
wrongExplanations.3: We never pop on left turns.

mcqChain.hint: Check the pop condition used for both chains.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity of this approach for n points?

options.label: O(n log n) due to sorting; the scans are linear.
O(n^2) due to nested popping loops.
O(n) because each point is visited once.
O(n^3) because we check all pairs and points.

mcqChain.explanation: Sorting dominates at O(n log n); each chain is built with amortized O(1) pops per point.

wrongExplanations.1: The while pops are amortized; total pops ≤ number of appends.
wrongExplanations.3: We do not check all pairs.

mcqChain.hint: Consider total number of pops across the whole run.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the additional space complexity (beyond input storage)?

options.label: O(n) for the chains and dedup structures.
O(1) extra, in-place.
O(n^2) for pairwise comparisons.
O(log n) for recursion.

mcqChain.explanation: We store lower, upper, hull, and sets/lists of size up to O(n).

wrongExplanations.1: We keep multiple lists of points; not constant space.

mcqChain.hint: We build two chains and a deduplicated list.

mcqChain.questionText: Which edge case is already guaranteed not to occur by the problem, but would otherwise need handling?

options.label: All points being collinear (hull area zero).
Duplicate points in input.
Coordinates outside 32-bit range.
An empty input file.

mcqChain.explanation: The problem guarantees positive area; otherwise special handling is needed for a line segment hull.

wrongExplanations.2: Python integers handle large coordinates; range is within constraints.
wrongExplanations.3: Input format always has at least n=3 points.

mcqChain.hint: Check the guarantee: positive hull area.

finalCodeSnippets:
def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    it = iter(data)
    n = int(next(it))

pts = [(int(next(it)), int(next(it))) for _ in range(n)]
    pts.sort()

out = []
    out.append(str(len(unique)))
    for x, y in unique:
        out.append(f"{x} {y}")
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

reasoningChain.question: What exactly must the output contain?
reasoningChain.insight: We must print all boundary points (including edge collinear points) and order does not matter.

reasoningChain.question: To systematically sweep the plane, which sorting of points is most useful?
reasoningChain.insight: Sorting by x then y allows a left-to-right sweep to build boundary chains.

reasoningChain.question: Given three points o, a, b, which expression gives the signed area and orientation?
reasoningChain.insight: Use the determinant cross product to detect left/right turns and collinearity.

reasoningChain.question: When building a chain, what to do on a right turn?
reasoningChain.insight: Pop the last point until the chain is not making a right turn to maintain convexity.

reasoningChain.question: How to include collinear edge points?
reasoningChain.insight: Use a strict '< 0' condition to pop only right turns, preserving collinear triples.

reasoningChain.question: How to form the full hull and avoid duplicates?
reasoningChain.insight: Build both chains (reverse for the second), slice off endpoints, concatenate, then deduplicate with a set.

reasoningChain.question: What is the time complexity and space usage?
reasoningChain.insight: Sorting dominates O(n log n); chain construction and dedup are linear; extra space is O(n).
